creator: gerald@pfeifer.com
text:
On Sat, 19 Jan 2013, Matthew Seaman wrote:
> Bother.  I can't repeat this in my tests. This is compiling with:
> :
> With INSTALL_AS_USER=yes and PKG_DBDIR=${HOME}/pkgng I get local.sqlite
> and repo.sqlite created with my own user and group, and pkg info runs fine:

Sorry, I just found the missing bit of information:  In my tests,
I had run `pkg add` without INSTALL_AS_USER.  When I added that,
as in `env INSTALL_AS_USER=1 pkg add`, things work as expected.

Now there are two reasons I had not set INSTALL_AS_USER:

 - With pkg_add, this was not necessary.  pkg_add just works as 
   long as the euid/eguid allow access to the "database".

 - INSTALL_AS_USER is not documented under `pkg help add`.

I'd like to make the case that instead of requiring the user to set
INSTALL_AS_USER in the environment, the original behaviour of just
going by file system access rights is preferable.  It consumes less
effort for the user and is less surprising, plus the new behavior is
a regression in a way.

> Perhaps the variables could be better named: euid, egid here are the
> expected user and group ownership of the various files and directories.
> I've changed them to fileowner and filegroup.

Thanks!

> In normal use both /var/db/pkg and all the .sqlite files pkg uses within
> it should be owned by root:wheel and only writeable by root.  In this
> case, allowing non-root to modify the pkg DB would be a security debacle
> just waiting to happen.

Totally agreed, but that is prevented by regular file system access
control in any (undesirable) case, isn't it?

> The change as you have it would mean that an ordinary user could not
> run pkg info on a standard system.

Bummer, you're right.

I had actually tested this with a newly built pkg on ref10-i386 and
got the following:

  % $PKG_BIN info
  pkg: The database is outdated and opened readonly

That's why I thought things were working.

> The install_as_user flag specifically changes the requirement on
> ownership so that a (non-root) user can have a personally owned copy of
> a pkgng database.  Implicit in that is the assumption that they're going
> to set PKG_DBDIR to put their private DB in other than the default
> location, and probably customize PREFIX similarly.

This is actually my use case. :-)  As part of my port testing scripts,
I am using exactly such a setup and this report, and some future ones,
are about my findings and unexpected changes moving from pkg_* to pkg.


The need to set INSTALL_AS_USER in the environment, when PKG_DBDIR
already points to a "personal" copy under my user id and one of my
groups, is surprising.

And in the case of groups, of which the average user has several, I
think this will even block access even when it would work out nicely
from a file system perspective.

(For example, if we had a special package tree for a project foo that
was guarded by a group foo that project admins would have as a secondary
group, it seems the current code will -- overeagerly! -- block that.
Even if we don't want to support that use case, creating a temporary
database in a subdirectory of /tmp will be blocked, since that grants
group wheel which I do not have on the FreeBSD.org cluster.)

I really believe the use of INSTALL_AS_USER needs to be removed (and
its use left to Mk/bsd.*.mk), though I realize it needs to be done
more diligently than with my previous patch. ;-)

Thanks,
Gerald
